file.content:
# Bugs

Known defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for.
Each entry describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of
the failure. Entries are numbered (`1.` `2.` …) the same way
as `## Todo` in `todo.md`; run
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/bugs.md` to renumber after
insert / delete / reorder.

## Bugs

1. **`finalize` squash silently drops the source journal's
   ochids.** `finalize_exec` (`src/finalize.rs`) always runs
   `jj squash --ignore-immutable --use-destination-message
   --from <source> --into <target>`, so when the source
   commit's message carries `ochid:` trailers the squash
   discards them with the rest of the message — the
   destination's message wins unconditionally.
   - **When it bites:** the squash source (`@`) is a
     *described* journal instead of a bare trailing-data
     snapshot. Observed sequence (fc op log, 2026-06-08):
     the bot wrote the journal message via
     `jj describe wyrlsuusnyzz` while that change was still
     the uncommitted `@`; finalize then squashed
     `--from @ --into @-` where `@-` was the previous,
     already-pushed journal — destination message won,
     journal message + 6 ochids discarded. Finalize's
     assumption (`@` is disposable, `@-` holds the real
     message) inverts whenever the journal is described on
     `@` rather than committed first.
   - **Cost:** every code-side commit pointing at the
     squashed journal is left with a dangling
     `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` — the code↔session cross-link
     breaks. Recovery requires op-log surgery on the machine
     that still has the original object (this happened in the
     fc project: journal `wyrlsuusnyzz`, 6 dangling app
     ochids, recovered 2026-06-10 from a backup workspace's
     op log plus a `.claude main` force-push).
   - **Fix direction:** before squashing, detect `ochid:`
     trailers in the source message that the destination
     lacks; refuse (or merge the trailers into the
     destination message) instead of dropping them.

2. **`finalize::surface_previous_failures` is racy and
   bounded by "next vc-x1 run".** The current model has
   four gaps:
   - **Stale forever.** Markers sit on disk until the
     next `vc-x1 <anything>` runs. If the user abandons
     the workspace (e.g., CI / scheduled use), failures
     are never surfaced.
   - **Concurrent surface_previous_failures.** Two
     `vc-x1` runs racing: both `read_dir`, both print
     the marker, one deletes (the other's `remove_file`
     silently fails). The user sees the same failure
     printed twice.
   - **Mid-write torn read.** A `finalize --exec` child
     writing a marker while a sibling `vc-x1` is
     surfacing could read partial content. Atomic-rename
     on write would close this.
   - **No notify-at-failure path.** A detached
     `finalize --exec` failure only becomes visible when
     the user next runs *any* `vc-x1` command — fine for
     interactive use, invisible for CI / scheduled use
     where there may be no next run.

   The exec-child gate in `main.rs` (since 0.52.0-3)
   patches one related case — the detached child eating
   its own prior markers before the user can see them —
   but doesn't address any of the above. Holistic fix
   needs locking + atomic writes + maybe a
   notify-at-failure path for hands-off use.

# References

input.new_string:
**feat: single-mode sync + revert command (0.67.0)**

`vc-x1 sync` defaults to `--check`, whose "verify only"
contract is a fiction (jj's fetch auto-fast-forwards tracked
bookmarks) and whose two-invocation verify-then-apply flow is
racy — the remote can move between the check run and the apply
run. Observed on test-repo-1: default sync reported "all
bookmarks up-to-date" while `@` stayed parented on the
pre-fetch tip. Sync becomes a single atomic operation with no
modes; failures stop for inspection instead of auto-reverting;
a new `revert` command undoes a sync explicitly.

   - 0.67.0-0 Preparation: backfill 0.66.0 Commits ref, bump
     version, write this block, open the chores section
     (current)
   - 0.67.0-1 tests: two-clone peer-push coverage — in-process
     `sync_clone_ffs_main_after_peer_push` plus end-to-end
     `tests/cli_sync.rs` (init → clone trA/trB → change +
     push on trA → sync on trB → main and @ move)
   - 0.67.0-2 sync single-mode: drop `--no-check`; default
     fetches, converges the bookmark, repositions `@`.
     `--check` stays as a hidden deprecated alias of the old
     verify-only mode until push preflight is rewired (its
     own Todo)
   - 0.67.0-3 stop-on-error: remove the auto `jj op restore`
     revert; persist the pre-sync op snapshot
     (`.vc-x1/sync-state.toml`); the error report names each
     repo + op id and points at `vc-x1 revert`
   - 0.67.0-4 `vc-x1 revert`: restore every repo to the
     persisted snapshot via `jj op restore`, then clear the
     state file
   - 0.67.0 close-out: chores narrative, README update, Done
     entry

   "put the bookmark / `@` where it belongs" step at the end
   of sync (reposition logic) is useful standalone — e.g. the
   t1B scenario where `main` is right but `@` isn't on it —
   and deserves an honestly-named command instead of a mode.

     (and optionally the bookmark) onto a target under the
     same safety rules as sync's reposition step.
   - Sync's final step becomes a call to the same logic.
   - Follow-up to the 0.67.0 single-mode sync cycle.
3. **sync follow-up: push preflight in-process; drop
   `--check`; revisit push auto-rollback.** Push's preflight
   shells out to `vc-x1 sync --check` — a verify-only pass
   that is both racy (remote can move before the user's

   auto-ffs tracked bookmarks). Follow-up to the 0.67.0

     halts the push before anything is committed); the

     push's commit-stage rollback (today it auto-runs
     `jj op restore`, hiding the evidence).

aiTitle: Test main bookmark update on vc-x1 sync

content.text: Now the two new bugs:

input.old_string: # References
input.new_string:
3. **`init --repo local` bare remotes keep HEAD at
   `refs/heads/master`.** The only branch pushed is `main`,
   so a later `jj git clone` of that bare repo has no default
   branch to auto-track and `vc-x1 clone` fails its
   `verify_tracking` check ("bookmark 'main' has non-tracking
   remote 'main@origin'"). Found building `tests/cli_sync.rs`
   (worked around there with `git symbolic-ref HEAD
   refs/heads/main`).
   - **Fix direction:** init's local bare provisioning sets
     HEAD to `refs/heads/main` at creation.

4. **`clone` session-remote derivation mismatches init's
   local naming; relative TARGET breaks the session clone.**
   Two related defects, both found building
   `tests/cli_sync.rs`:
   - `derive_session_url` maps `<x>.git` →
     `<x>.claude.git`, but `init --repo local` names the
     session remote `remote-claude.git` — so a dual clone of
     a locally-init'd project's `remote-code.git` looks for
     `remote-code.claude.git` and fails.
   - A relative local-path TARGET fails on the session side
     regardless: `clone_dual` runs the session `jj git
     clone` with the just-cloned code repo as cwd, so the
     relative source no longer resolves. Workaround: pass an
     absolute TARGET.

# References

     breaks. Recovery requires op-log surgery on the machine
     that still has the original object (this happened in the
     fc project: journal `wyrlsuusnyzz`, 6 dangling app
     ochids, recovered 2026-06-10 from a backup workspace's
     op log plus a `.claude main` force-push).
   - **Fix direction:** before squashing, detect `ochid:`
     trailers in the source message that the destination
     lacks; refuse (or merge the trailers into the
     destination message) instead of dropping them.

2. **`finalize::surface_previous_failures` is racy and
   bounded by "next vc-x1 run".** The current model has
   four gaps:
   - **Stale forever.** Markers sit on disk until the
     next `vc-x1 <anything>` runs. If the user abandons
     the workspace (e.g., CI / scheduled use), failures
     are never surfaced.
   - **Concurrent surface_previous_failures.** Two
     `vc-x1` runs racing: both `read_dir`, both print
     the marker, one deletes (the other's `remove_file`
     silently fails). The user sees the same failure
     printed twice.
   - **Mid-write torn read.** A `finalize --exec` child
     writing a marker while a sibling `vc-x1` is
     surfacing could read partial content. Atomic-rename
     on write would close this.
   - **No notify-at-failure path.** A detached
     `finalize --exec` failure only becomes visible when
     the user next runs *any* `vc-x1` command — fine for
     interactive use, invisible for CI / scheduled use
     where there may be no next run.

   The exec-child gate in `main.rs` (since 0.52.0-3)
   patches one related case — the detached child eating
   its own prior markers before the user can see them —
   but doesn't address any of the above. Holistic fix
   needs locking + atomic writes + maybe a
   notify-at-failure path for hands-off use.

# References

126	    // vc-x1 clone <code-remote> trB / trA (B first, so its

146	    // stops before finalize-claude (out of scope here, and its

149	    fs::write(tr_a.join("from-a.txt"), "from clone A\n").expect("write trA change");
150	    run_ok(fx.cmd().current_dir(&tr_a).env("PATH", test_path()).args([
151	        "push",
152	        "main",
153	        "--from",
154	        "review",
155	        "--title",
156	        "feat: from clone A",
157	        "--body",
158	        "add from-a.txt",

162	    let pushed = cid(&fx.home, &tr_a, "main");

content.text:
Found it — it's not an environment variable, it's your cargo config: `~/.cargo/config.toml:7` has `rustc-wrapper = "sccache"`. The `RUSTC_WRAPPER` env var *overrides* that config entry, and setting it to empty disables the wrapper entirely, so cargo calls `rustc` directly.

I reached for it when `cargo test` failed with `sccache: error: Operation not permitted` under the session sandbox — sccache talks to a background server over a socket outside the sandbox's writable paths. One note: a later run with the sandbox disabled *also* hit the sccache error, which suggests the sccache server got into a bad state (the bot thinks it was started/probed under the sandbox and is now wedged). If plain `cargo build` fails for you in a normal terminal, `sccache --stop-server` should clear it; the next cargo invocation restarts it fresh.

For my remaining runs I'll keep using `RUSTC_WRAPPER=` — it's per-invocation, touches nothing on disk, and just means those builds skip the compile cache.

content.text:
Good — so sccache is fine on your side, and the failure was purely the sandbox blocking its socket. I'll keep the `RUSTC_WRAPPER=` override only for sandboxed runs and stop speculating about the server.

Back to where we stood, the cycle state is:

- **Failing test demonstrated**: `cli_sync_default_moves_main_and_at_after_peer_push` — your exact invocation (plain `vc-x1 sync` on trB after trA pushed) — fails today exactly as in your transcript: `main` moved but `@-` stayed on the pre-fetch tip. It's `#[ignore]`d with reason "fails until 0.67.0-2 single-mode sync", so the suite stays green; `cargo test -- --ignored` shows the red. The passing `--no-check` variant shares the same setup/assert helpers, so the only difference between the two tests is the flag.
- **Preparation (`0.67.0-0`) edits ready for review**: chores `Commits:` backfill + new section header, `## In Progress` block with the ladder, two follow-up Todos, two new bugs.md entries, version bump to `0.67.0-0`.

I'd commit `-0` with just the prep files (`Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`, `notes/todo.md`, `notes/bugs.md`, `notes/chores/chores-13.md`), leaving both test files in `@` for `-1`. Ready to commit — please review.

message.content: What is your definition of a "sandboxed run"?

content.text:
A "sandboxed run" is any shell command I execute through my Bash tool while the session's sandbox is on — which it has been since you ran `/sandbox` earlier ("Sandbox enabled with auto-allow"). The sandbox is an OS-level confinement wrapped around each command I run, with two kinds of restrictions:

- **Filesystem:** reads are broadly allowed, but *writes* are limited to an allowlist — essentially this project directory, my session scratchpad under `/tmp/claude-1000/...`, and a few housekeeping paths. Anything else looks like a read-only filesystem to the command. That's what produced the two failures you saw: jj trying to create `~/.config/jj/repos/<id>` (its per-repo "secure config") got `Read-only file system`, and sccache got `Operation not permitted` talking to its server socket.
- **Network:** only an allowlisted set of hosts (github.com, crates.io, docs.rs, etc.).

Two important boundaries of the definition:

- It applies to **my tool invocations only**. Your terminal, your `cargo build`, and anything you run yourself are never sandboxed — which is exactly why your `cargo clean; cargo build` worked fine while my identical command failed.
- It's **per-command escapable**: I can run a specific command unsandboxed, which asks your permission (that's how the full test suite passed earlier — the in-process sync tests spawn jj, which needs to write `~/.config/jj/repos`).

So concretely for this project: sandboxed runs are fine for cargo *builds* if I disable the sccache wrapper (`RUSTC_WRAPPER=`), but any test that shells out to jj against a fresh repo needs either an unsandboxed run or a `HOME` override into the fixture (which is what `tests/cli_sync.rs` does — one of the reasons its helpers set `HOME` explicitly).

I did that because I complained to claude-web that I was annoyed that
for every command I had to approve when you used $PWD and that was a
suggested fix. Is there a better way?

content.text:
Yes — there's a mechanism aimed at exactly this. The prompts you were seeing come from Claude Code's permission system: every Bash command is matched against allow/deny rules in `.claude/settings.json`, and anything unmatched asks you. Compound commands (`cd … && …`, `$PWD` interpolations, pipelines) are especially prompt-prone because they don't match simple prefix rules.

The sandbox-with-auto-allow you enabled is one fix — "anything sandboxed runs without asking" — but as this session showed, it trades prompts for breakage in jj-heavy work (jj wants to write `~/.config/jj/repos`, sccache wants its socket), and then I end up asking you to approve *unsandboxed* escapes instead, so the prompt count doesn't really drop for this project.

The better fit is a **permission allowlist**: add `Bash(jj *)`, `Bash(cargo *)`, etc. rules to this project's `.claude/settings.json` so the commands this workflow actually uses run without prompting, sandbox off. It's reviewable, committed with the repo, and scoped — anything outside the list still asks. There's a built-in helper for it: the `/fewer-permission-prompts` skill scans my past transcripts in this project for the read-only commands I actually run and writes a prioritized allowlist into `.claude/settings.json`.

Two caveats worth knowing:

- Allowlists match commands, so mutating tools you trust here (`jj commit`, `cargo test`, `vc-x1 …`) can be listed too — that's your call on how permissive to be; the skill leans read-only and we can add the rest by hand.
- Keeping the sandbox on *as well* is fine in principle, but for this project I'd turn it off — the test harness spawns jj against fresh repos constantly, and each one trips the write restriction.

Want me to run `/fewer-permission-prompts` and then hand-add the project's workhorse commands (`jj`, `cargo`, `vc-x1`) for you to review?

# Fewer Permission Prompts

Look through my transcripts' MCP and bash tool calls, and based on those, make a prioritized list of patterns that I should add to my permission allowlist to reduce permission prompts. Focus on read-only commands.

The format for permissions is: `Bash(foo*)`, `Bash(foo)`, `Bash(foo bar *)`, `mcp__slack__slack_read_thread`, etc.

Then, add these to the project `.claude/settings.json` under `permissions.allow`.

## Steps

1. **Locate transcripts.** Session transcripts live at `~/.claude/projects/<sanitized-cwd>/*.jsonl`. Each line is a JSON object. Tool calls appear as `assistant` messages with `message.content[]` entries of `type: "tool_use"`. The `name` field identifies the tool (e.g. `"Bash"`, `"mcp__slack__slack_read_thread"`); for Bash, `input.command` is the shell string.

   Scan the recent transcripts across the user's projects dir — not just the current project — so the allowlist reflects their actual usage. Cap the scan at a reasonable number of recent sessions (e.g. 50 most-recently-modified JSONL files) so this stays fast.

2. **Extract tool-call frequencies.**
   - For `Bash` calls: parse `input.command`, take the leading command token (handling `sudo`, `timeout`, pipes, `&&`, env-var prefixes). Record the command + first subcommand pair (e.g. `git status`, `gh pr view`, `ls`, `cat`).
   - For MCP calls: record the full tool name (e.g. `mcp__slack__slack_read_thread`).
   - Count occurrences across the scanned transcripts.

3. **Filter to read-only.** Keep only commands that don't mutate state. Examples of read-only: `ls`, `cat`, `pwd`, `git status`, `git log`, `git diff`, `git show`, `git branch`, `rg`, `grep`, `find`, `head`, `tail`, `wc`, `file`, `which`, `echo`, `date`, `gh pr view`, `gh pr list`, `gh pr diff`, `gh issue view`, `gh issue list`, `gh run list`, `gh run view`, `gh api` (GET), `bun run typecheck`, `bun run lint`, `bun run test` (for tests that don't mutate), `docker ps`, `docker logs`, `kubectl get`, `kubectl describe`, `ps`, `top`, `df`, `du`, `env`, `printenv`, any MCP tool with `read`/`get`/`list`/`search`/`view` in its name.

   Drop anything that writes, deletes, renames, pushes, merges, installs, or runs a build/test that has side effects. When in doubt, leave it out.

   **Never allowlist a pattern that grants arbitrary code execution.** A wildcard rule for any of these (e.g. `Bash(python3:*)`) is equivalent to allowing arbitrary code execution. This list is not exhaustive — apply the same rule to anything in the same category:
   - Interpreters: `python`/`python3`, `node`, `bun`, `deno`, `ruby`, `perl`, `php`, `lua`, etc.
   - Shells: `bash`, `sh`, `zsh`, `fish`, `eval`, `exec`, `ssh`, etc.
   - Package runners: `npx`, `bunx`, `uvx`, `uv run`, etc.
   - Task-runner wildcards: `npm run *`, `yarn run *`, `pnpm run *`, `bun run *`, `make *`, `just *`, `cargo run *`, `go run *`, etc. — an exact `Bash(bun run typecheck)` is fine, `Bash(bun run *)` is not
   - `gh api *`, `docker run`/`exec`, `kubectl exec`, `sudo`, and similar

4. **Drop commands Claude Code already auto-allows.** These don't need an allowlist entry — they never prompt. If you see any of these in the transcripts, skip them; don't suggest them to the user.

   - **Always auto-allowed (any args):** `cal`, `uptime`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `wc`, `stat`, `strings`, `hexdump`, `od`, `nl`, `id`, `uname`, `free`, `df`, `du`, `locale`, `groups`, `nproc`, `basename`, `dirname`, `realpath`, `cut`, `paste`, `tr`, `column`, `tac`, `rev`, `fold`, `expand`, `unexpand`, `fmt`, `comm`, `cmp`, `numfmt`, `readlink`, `diff`, `true`, `false`, `sleep`, `which`, `type`, `expr`, `seq`, `tsort`, `pr`, `echo`, `ls`, `cd`.
   - **Auto-allowed with zero args only:** `pwd`, `whoami`, `alias`.
   - **Auto-allowed exact forms:** `claude -h`, `claude --help`, `node -v`, `node --version`, `python --version`, `python3 --version`, `ip addr`.
   - **Auto-allowed with safe flags only (validated):** `xargs`, `file`, `sed` (read-only expressions), `sort`, `man`, `help`, `netstat`, `ps`, `base64`, `grep`, `egrep`, `fgrep`, `sha256sum`, `sha1sum`, `md5sum`, `tree`, `date`, `hostname`, `lsof`, `pgrep`, `tput`, `ss`, `fd`, `fdfind`, `aki`, `rg`, `jq`, `uniq`, `history`, `arch`, `ifconfig`, `pyright`, `find` (blocks `-delete`/`-exec`/`-execdir`/`-ok`/`-okdir`/`-fprint*`/`-fls`/`-files0-from`), `printf` (blocks any `-flag`), `test` (blocks `-v`/`-R`/`-a`/`-o`).
   - **All git read-only subcommands:** `git status`, `git log`, `git diff`, `git show`, `git blame`, `git branch`, `git tag`, `git remote`, `git ls-files`, `git ls-remote`, `git config --get`, `git rev-parse`, `git describe`, `git stash list`, `git reflog`, `git shortlog`, `git cat-file`, `git for-each-ref`, `git worktree list`, etc.
   - **All gh read-only subcommands:** `gh pr view`, `gh pr list`, `gh pr diff`, `gh pr checks`, `gh pr status`, `gh issue view`, `gh issue list`, `gh issue status`, `gh run view`, `gh run list`, `gh workflow list`, `gh workflow view`, `gh repo view`, `gh release view`, `gh release list`, `gh api` (GET), `gh auth status`, etc.
   - **Docker read-only subcommands:** `docker ps`, `docker images`, `docker logs`, `docker inspect`.

   Source of truth: `src/tools/BashTool/readOnlyValidation.ts` (`READONLY_COMMANDS`, `READONLY_NOARGS`, `READONLY_EXACT`, `COMMAND_ALLOWLIST`) and `src/utils/shell/readOnlyCommandValidation.ts` (`GIT_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`, `GH_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`, `DOCKER_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`, `RIPGREP_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`, `PYRIGHT_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`). If the user is in this repo and you're unsure whether a command is covered, grep these files rather than guessing.

5. **Pick the pattern form.** Use the narrowest pattern that still covers the observed usage:
   - If the user runs many variants (`git log`, `git log --oneline`, `git log main..HEAD`): use `Bash(git log *)` — note the space before `*`, which is required for prefix matching to work correctly.
   - If a single exact invocation is common: use `Bash(foo)` with no wildcard.
   - For MCP: use the full tool name verbatim (no wildcard needed; they're already specific).
   - Never widen a pattern to the point that it conflicts with the rules above (no arbitrary code execution, no mutation/side effects).

6. **Prioritize.** Rank by count descending. Drop anything that appeared fewer than ~3 times — not worth the allowlist entry. Cap the list at the top ~20 so the user can skim it.

7. **Present the prioritized list to the user** as a markdown table with columns: rank, pattern, count, one-line description. Example:

   | # | Pattern | Count | Notes |
   |---|---------|-------|-------|
   | 1 | `Bash(git status *)` | 142 | repo status checks |
   | 2 | `Bash(gh pr view *)` | 87 | PR inspection |
   | 3 | `mcp__slack__slack_read_thread` | 54 | Slack thread reads |

8. **Merge into `.claude/settings.json`** in the current project (not `~/.claude/settings.json`, not `.claude/settings.local.json`). Create the file if it doesn't exist. Preserve existing keys and existing entries in `permissions.allow`; de-duplicate against what's already there; don't remove anything; don't reorder unrelated fields.

9. **Report back.** Tell the user what you added (count + a few examples), what was already in the allowlist, and what you skipped and why (e.g. "dropped `rm` and `git push` — not read-only; dropped `cat`/`ls`/`git status` — already auto-allowed, no rule needed").

Do not add anything to `permissions.deny` or `permissions.ask`. Do not touch any other settings field.
Yes — once the allowlist is in place, turning `/sandbox` off is the right move for this project. Now scanning transcripts:

input.command: ls -t ~/.claude/projects/*/*.jsonl | head -50 | xargs -d '\n' cat 2>/dev/null | jq -r 'select(.type=="assistant") | .message.content[]? | select(.type=="tool_use" and .name=="Bash") | .input.command' 2>/dev/null | sed -E 's/^[A-Z_]+=[^ ]* //; s/^(sudo|timeout [0-9]+s?) //' | awk '{if ($1=="cargo"||$1=="git"||$1=="gh"||$1=="jj"||$1=="vc-x1"||$1=="docker"||$1=="kubectl") print $1, $2; else print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -40

message.content: What should we do aoubt `"autoAllowBashIfSandboxed": true` since we're going to turn it off? But if `$PWD` are going to byte me again I'll leave it?

content.text:
Leave it. `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed` is inert while `sandbox.enabled` is `false` — it only means "when the sandbox *is* on, don't prompt for sandboxed commands". Keeping it `true` just preserves your preference: if you ever flip the sandbox back on (`/sandbox`), it comes back in the no-prompt mode you liked, with zero effect in the meantime.

On `$PWD` biting you again: those prompts weren't caused by `$PWD` itself — a command like `jj st -R $PWD` is matched as a plain string against the allow patterns, and `Bash(jj *)` covers it fine. What actually falls through the patterns is: compound commands (`cd x && jj …`), env-var prefixes (`RUSTC_WRAPPER= cargo …`), and commands not starting with an allowed token. The new allowlist (`jj`, `cargo`, `vc-x1`, `./target/*/vc-x1`) covers this project's bread and butter, and the project conventions already steer me away from `cd`-compounds. If some shape still prompts repeatedly, we add that one pattern rather than re-enabling the sandbox.

So: `settings.json` (committed) has the five allow rules; `settings.local.json` has the sandbox off with auto-allow preserved for the future. If that looks right, we're back at the `0.67.0-0` Preparation commit, which is still awaiting your review — prep files only (`Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`, `notes/todo.md`, `notes/bugs.md`, `notes/chores/chores-13.md`), with the test files (including the new ignored failing test) left in `@` for `-1`.

content.text:
You're right — I said "committed" loosely. Since this project keeps `.claude/` as its own jj repo, `settings.json` is a new tracked file in the *session* repo's working copy, and it'll actually get recorded when the next session commit lands (the finalize at the next `vc-x1 push`). So it ends up versioned and pushed with the journal — which is what we want for the shared allowlist — just via the `.claude` repo rather than the app repo.

One thing your `jj st` reveals that's worth a decision: `settings.local.json` is *also* tracked (shows as `M`). That file is meant to be machine-local, personal state — it's where "Approve always" answers and the sandbox toggle accumulate — and Claude Code's convention elsewhere is to gitignore it. Tracked, it means every stray one-off approval you ever click gets committed and pushed with the session repo. If you'd rather keep it local-only, I can add a `.claude/.gitignore` entry for `settings.local.json` and `jj file untrack` it. Or leave it tracked if you like having that history in the journal — your call; functionally both work.

message.content: I work on different machines and kinda expect the same behavior on all of them, which means I want to commit it, but that's how I want to work others might have different opinions.

I work on different machines and kinda expect the same behavior on all of them, which means I want to commit it, but that's how I want to work others might have different opinions. And I see that it's huge, not sure what to do, thoughts?

content.text:
Good instinct on both counts. The size problem and the sync-across-machines goal actually point to the same cleanup: the file is huge because it's a *sediment layer* — every time you clicked "approve always", Claude Code appended that exact command as a rule. Look at what's in there: `Bash(sed -n '1483,1486p' src/push.rs)`, `Bash(echo "exit=$?")`… rules that will never match anything again. Maybe 10 of the 90 entries carry durable intent, and the new wildcards in `settings.json` subsume most of those.

My suggestion — use the two files by *role*, not by accident of history:

- **`settings.json` = the durable, deliberate policy** (tracked in the `.claude` repo, so every machine that clones the project gets it). Migrate the keepers from local into it: `WebSearch`, the `WebFetch(domain:…)` entries (github.com, docs.jj-vcs.dev, crates.io, docs.rs, raw.githubusercontent.com, docs.kernel.org, claude.ai), `Read(//tmp/**)`, and the already-added `jj`/`cargo`/`vc-x1` wildcards. Maybe `Bash(awk *)` and `Bash(bash notes/substep-test.sh)` if you want them.
- **`settings.local.json` = disposable scratch** where future one-off approvals land. Prune it now to almost nothing (the sandbox block can go entirely — off is the default, and `/sandbox` recreates it on demand). Accept that it regrows, and sweep it into `settings.json` or delete entries occasionally — same spirit as your `## Done` → `done.md` migration beat.

Tracking the local file is then harmless — it stays small between sweeps, and committing it gives you the same behavior everywhere, which is what you want. (The name "local" stops being accurate for your workflow, but the mechanics don't care: both files' `permissions.allow` lists are unioned.)

Want me to do that migration and prune now?

message.content: Based on what you say it should be added to .gitignore, but in reality this dual repo setup is my "invention", the intention of Anthropic is that these are both "local" and saved in ~/.claude/projects/, correct?

content.text:
Close, but the standard design is a bit different — it splits by *purpose*, and your invention changes where one half lives:

- **`~/.claude/projects/<sanitized-path>/`** — this is what Anthropic intends as machine-local: session *data* (transcripts like those `.jsonl` files, session state). Never versioned, never shared.
- **`<project>/.claude/settings.json`** — intended to live in the project and be **checked into the project's own repo**, shared with collaborators. In a stock setup it'd be a normal tracked file of the app repo.
- **`<project>/.claude/settings.local.json`** — intended personal-per-project; stock Claude Code even auto-adds it to the app repo's `.gitignore` for you.
- **`~/.claude/settings.json`** — user-global, machine-local.

So Anthropic's intent isn't "both settings files are local under `~/.claude/projects/`" — settings were always meant to sit in the project, with the `.json`/`.local.json` split being shared-vs-personal. What your dual-repo invention changes is that `<project>/.claude/` is itself a repo (with `~/.claude/projects/<path>` symlinked into it), so *everything* in it — session journal **and** both settings files — gets versioned and pushed in the session repo instead of settings riding in the app repo.

That makes your earlier call coherent: in your workflow the "personal machine-local" category barely exists — you *want* one behavior across machines, and the `.claude` repo is precisely your mechanism for syncing per-project bot state. So ignoring `settings.local.json` would follow stock convention but fight your design. Tracking both, with the roles we discussed (`settings.json` = curated policy, `settings.local.json` = approval sediment, swept periodically), is the consistent choice for this setup — someone with the stock one-repo layout would instead gitignore the local file, which is the "others might have different opinions" case you anticipated.

Shall I do the migration/prune of the two files now, and then we get back to the pending `0.67.0-0` commit?

message.content: We'll do the pruning but I wonder what the goal should be. My thought is that saving the conversation with the code allows a much better historical record and allows the possibility to answer, why did the user do xyz. That is the whole idea and intention of this dual repo mode. And vc-x1 is the tool that might someday allow you to explore the connection made possible by the ochid's. Thoughts?

content.text:
I think the idea is genuinely sound, and this very session is a good exhibit for it. Consider what the app repo will record of 0.67.0: a chores section saying sync became single-mode, stop-on-error, with a revert command. What it *can't* record is how that decision actually formed: your t1A/t1B transcript, my two wrong reproductions (I tested `--no-check` because I "knew how to use the program"), the realization that check-mode's read-only contract was already a fiction, your rejection of verify-then-apply as inherently racy, then of auto-revert as evidence-destroying. That derivation — including the dead ends — is the real "why did the user do xyz", and it exists only in the conversation. Commit messages are the compressed verdict; the journal is the trial record.

The ochid is what makes this more than an archive. A code commit carrying `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` is a durable, rebase-surviving pointer from *change* to *deliberation* — every clone can resolve it. What's missing today is exactly the tool you're gesturing at: the link lands you on a journal commit, which is a large `.jsonl` blob; nobody will read that raw. The future vc-x1 capability is the dereference: given a code commit, resolve its ochid, extract the conversation slice that produced it (the transcripts are timestamped, so a commit can be mapped to a window of turns), maybe summarize or index it. Then "why?" becomes a query instead of an act of archaeology. Notably, bug #1 in bugs.md — the finalize squash dropping ochids — is exactly the failure mode that severs this chain, which is why guarding those trailers (0.65.2) mattered more than it looked.

Two honest caveats. First, the record is raw and enormous — its value depends on that future dereference tool existing; until then you're banking evidence at low cost, which jj/git handles fine. Second, publishing deliberation is a real choice: transcripts contain everything (paths, mistakes, half-formed ideas), and a public `.claude` repo shares it all. You've clearly accepted that; a collaborator might not — same "others might have different opinions" boundary as the settings question.

And that gives the pruning question its principle: **the journal is the record of deliberation; settings are policy.** Transcripts belong in history verbatim — never prune those. `settings.local.json`'s approval sediment isn't deliberation, it's UI residue; sweeping it into a curated `settings.json` loses no "why" (and the sweep itself gets recorded in the journal anyway). So the goal for pruning: keep the record complete, keep the policy legible.

Shall I do the sweep now — migrate the keepers into `settings.json`, empty the sediment out of `settings.local.json`, drop the sandbox block — and then we return to the pending `0.67.0-0` commit?

content.text:
Good catch — that's exactly the right suspect, and it's a real defect in the 0.66.0-vintage test, not flakiness. Diagnosis:

`sync_code_skips_rebase_without_flag` drives `sync_repos` **in-process** with a non-empty `@` and no `--rebase`, which lands in `confirm_rebase` (src/sync.rs:542). That function's guard is "prompt only if stdin is a terminal" — and when *you* run `cargo test` in a real terminal, the test harness inherits your TTY stdin, so it blocks on `read_line`. Your enter key answers "" → "no" → the skip path continues and the test passes. It never hung for me only because my shell tool's stdin isn't a TTY, so `is_terminal()` returned false and the prompt was skipped — an environment-dependent test.

The minimal deterministic fix: make `confirm_rebase` treat the test harness like a non-interactive session:

Fixed and green on my side — but the real proof needs your terminal, since the hang only reproduces with a TTY stdin. Please run `cargo test` in your shell; it should now complete with no pause (the `cfg!(test)` arm makes the prompt unreachable in test builds, so it can't block regardless of TTY).

Updated scope of the `-1` commit (one file added since your last review):

- **`src/sync.rs`** — `confirm_rebase` now returns the non-interactive "no" under `cfg!(test)`, with a doc-comment bullet explaining the harness-inherits-TTY hang; this is the fix for the hang you found in `sync_code_skips_rebase_without_flag` (a 0.66.0-vintage latent bug — my runs never prompted because my tool's stdin isn't a terminal).
- `tests/cli_sync.rs`, `src/sync/integration_tests.rs`, todo.md markers, version `0.67.0-1` — as reviewed before.

Also noted while checking for siblings: `push` gates its prompts the same way (`interactive()` at `src/push.rs:546`), so the same latent hazard exists there if an in-process test ever reaches a prompt path without `--yes`; its current tests don't. Worth a line in a future cycle rather than this commit.

Once your `cargo test` confirms clean, I'll write up the commit description.

That's weird, why is that?

content.text:
`-j` doesn't do what it looks like there: it controls **cargo's build parallelism** — how many compilation jobs run concurrently — not test execution. Once a test binary is built, cargo hands off to Rust's libtest harness, which schedules tests on its own thread pool (default = number of CPUs). To serialize the *tests* you pass flags through to the harness, after the `--` separator:

```
cargo test -- --test-threads=1
```

(or `RUST_TEST_THREADS=1 cargo test`). You can combine them: `cargo test -j 1 -- --test-threads=1` would build serially *and* run tests serially — two independent knobs for two different phases.

And yes — the un-ignored test failing with `@-` left on the old tip is exactly the red we want until `0.67.0-2` lands. One bookkeeping consequence: you edited `tests/cli_sync.rs` to remove the `#[ignore]`, and that file is part of the pending `-1` commit, so it needs to go back before we commit (the suite must be green at `-1`). Let me check the working copy:

content.text:
Two separate libtest behaviors explain that:

1. **A failing test doesn't stop the run.** libtest always runs every scheduled test and reports the tally at the end — there's no fail-fast by default (serial vs parallel doesn't change that; `--test-threads=1` only made them take turns, hence slower). A stable fail-fast option doesn't exist yet; people who want it usually just rerun the one test by name.
2. **The ignored test ran at all** because your invocation must have included `--include-ignored` (that's also why the summary says `0 ignored`). `#[ignore]` means "skip by default", not "never run": plain `cargo test` skips it and reports `1 ignored`, `cargo test -- --ignored` runs *only* the ignored ones, `--include-ignored` runs everything. That's the mechanism the plan leans on — the red test stays in the tree, visible on demand, and flips to always-run when `-2` removes the attribute.

So everything you're seeing is expected: green suite by default, deliberate red reachable explicitly. The `-1` commit description is in my previous message awaiting your OK.

1	//! Integration tests for the sync module.
2	//!
3	//! End-to-end tests for `sync_repos` against real dual-repo jj
4	//! fixtures. Each test builds an isolated fixture (bare-git
5	//! remotes + colocated jj repos) under a unique tempdir via
6	//! `Fixture::new`, then drives the scenario with plain `jj`
7	//! subprocess calls. Requires `jj` in `PATH`.
8	//!
9	//! Fixtures clean themselves up via `Fixture`'s `Drop` impl so a
10	//! panicking test still removes its tempdir.
11	
12	use super::*;
13	use crate::options_flags::scope::Side;
14	use crate::test_helpers::Fixture;
15	use std::fs;
16	use std::process::Command;
17	
18	use crate::common::{default_scope, find_workspace_root_from, scope_to_repos};
19	
20	/// Resolver helpers must consume what `init --repo-local` produces.
21	///
22	/// Builds a real dual-repo fixture (bare git remotes + colocated jj
23	/// repos + the canonical `.vc-config.toml` pair init writes), then
24	/// drives the resolver chain `sync()` uses when `-R` is empty:
25	///
26	/// - `find_workspace_root_from(&fx.claude)` walks up to `fx.work`
27	///   (proves cwd-portability against init's `path = "/.claude"` /
28	///   `path = "/"` config split).
29	/// - `default_scope(Some(&fx.work))` reads the workspace config and
30	///   resolves to the dual-repo default.
31	/// - `scope_to_repos` maps each `Scope` shape to the right
32	///   absolute path(s) under the fixture.
33	///
34	/// Pure check on the resolver chain — does not invoke `sync()`
35	/// itself, since that walks `std::env::current_dir()` and parallel
36	/// `cargo test` makes cwd mutation unsafe.
37	#[test]
38	fn resolver_chain_against_init_repo_local() {
39	    let fx = Fixture::new("resolver-chain");
40	
41	    // Walk-up from the bot side lands on the app root.
42	    assert_eq!(
43	        find_workspace_root_from(&fx.claude).as_deref(),
44	        Some(&*fx.work),
45	        "find_workspace_root should resolve from .claude up to work"
46	    );
47	    // Walk-up from the app root finds itself.
48	    assert_eq!(
49	        find_workspace_root_from(&fx.work).as_deref(),
50	        Some(&*fx.work)
51	    );
52	
53	    // init --repo-local writes other-repo = ".claude", so the
54	    // workspace's default scope is dual.
55	    assert_eq!(
56	        default_scope(Some(&fx.work)),
57	        Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot])
58	    );
59	
60	    // Each scope shape resolves to the right absolute path(s).
61	    assert_eq!(
62	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
63	        vec![fx.work.clone(), fx.claude.clone()]
64	    );
65	    assert_eq!(
66	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
67	        vec![fx.work.clone()]
68	    );
69	    assert_eq!(
70	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
71	        vec![fx.claude.clone()]
72	    );
73	
74	    // sync_repos accepts the resolved list and reports up-to-date
75	    // — the resolver's output is shaped the way sync expects.
76	    let resolved = scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap();
77	    sync_repos(&resolved, &default_params()).expect("sync should succeed on resolved repos");
78	}
79	
80	/// Run `jj <args> -R <repo>` and assert success; returns trimmed stdout.
81	fn jj(repo: &Path, args: &[&str]) -> String {
82	    let out = Command::new("jj")
83	        .args(args)
84	        .arg("-R")
85	        .arg(repo)
86	        .output()
87	        .expect("spawn jj");
88	    assert!(
89	        out.status.success(),
90	        "jj {args:?} failed in {}: {}",
91	        repo.display(),
92	        String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stderr)
93	    );
94	    String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stdout).trim().to_string()
95	}
96	
97	/// Resolve `rev` to its short commit id in `repo`.
98	fn cid(repo: &Path, rev: &str) -> String {
99	    jj(
100	        repo,
101	        &["log", "-r", rev, "--no-graph", "-T", "commit_id.short(12)"],
102	    )
103	}
104	
105	/// Default sync params (the normal atomic sync — no flags).
106	///
107	/// Integration tests pass explicit repo paths through `sync_repos`
108	/// directly, so `repo` / `scope` stay `None` here and the CLI-side
109	/// default resolution is not exercised by this helper.
110	fn default_params() -> SyncParams {
111	    SyncParams {
112	        check: false,
113	        quiet: false,
114	        bookmark: "main".to_string(),
115	        remote: "origin".to_string(),
116	        rebase: false,
117	        repo: None,
118	        scope: None,
119	    }
120	}
121	
122	/// Default params with `--rebase` set (auto-confirm the code-repo
123	/// non-empty `@` rebase).
124	fn rebase_params() -> SyncParams {
125	    SyncParams {
126	        rebase: true,
127	        ..default_params()
128	    }
129	}
130	
131	/// True when `revset` matches at least one commit in `repo`.
132	fn has(repo: &Path, revset: &str) -> bool {
133	    !jj(
134	        repo,
135	        &[
136	            "log",
137	            "-r",
138	            revset,
139	            "--no-graph",
140	            "-T",
141	            r#"commit_id.short(12) ++ "\n""#,
142	        ],
143	    )
144	    .trim()
145	    .is_empty()
146	}
147	
148	/// Add a local-only commit on `main` in `repo` (not pushed), then
149	/// restore `@` to an empty child so pre-flight still passes.
150	///
151	/// Sequence: write file → describe `@` → advance `main` to `@` →
152	/// create a fresh empty `@` above it.
153	fn add_local_commit(repo: &Path, file: &str, content: &str, msg: &str) {
154	    fs::write(repo.join(file), content).expect("write local file");
155	    jj(repo, &["describe", "@", "-m", msg]);
156	    jj(repo, &["bookmark", "set", "main", "-r", "@"]);
157	    jj(repo, &["new"]);
158	}
159	
160	/// Clone `remote_url` into `<base>/<work_name>` (colocated) and
161	/// return the new workdir.
162	fn clone(base: &Path, remote_url: &Path, work_name: &str) -> PathBuf {
163	    let workdir = base.join(work_name);
164	    let out = Command::new("jj")
165	        .args(["git", "clone", "--colocate"])
166	        .arg(remote_url)
167	        .arg(&workdir)
168	        .output()
169	        .expect("spawn jj clone");
170	    assert!(
171	        out.status.success(),
172	        "jj git clone failed: {}",
173	        String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stderr)
174	    );
175	    workdir
176	}
177	
178	/// Clone `remote_url` into `<base>/<work_name>`, add a commit, push it.
179	///
180	/// Used to make the remote advance beyond the fixture's `main`

182	fn push_from_clone(
183	    base: &Path,
184	    remote_url: &Path,
185	    work_name: &str,
186	    file: &str,
187	    content: &str,
188	    msg: &str,
189	) -> String {
190	    let workdir = clone(base, remote_url, work_name);

192	    jj(&workdir, &["describe", "@", "-m", msg]);

194	    jj(&workdir, &["git", "push", "--bookmark", "main"]);
195	    cid(&workdir, "main")
196	}
197	
198	/// Scenario 1: fresh fixture, nothing to do — `sync` leaves both
199	/// repos untouched.

/// Create a new dual-repo project
    Init(init::InitArgs),

    /// Create Claude Code project symlink
    Symlink(symlink::SymlinkArgs),

    /// Fetch and sync a set of repos to their remotes
    #[command(long_about = "Fetch and sync a set of repos to their remotes.\n\n\
        Repo set is resolved (in order):\n  \
          - `-R` / `--repo`     exact list (back-compat / arbitrary multi-repo)\n  \
          - `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` dual-repo roles via `.vc-config.toml`\n  \
          - neither             default: `code,bot` when dual, else `code`\n\n\
        Default is --check (verify only) — fatal if any repo needs\n\
        action. Re-run with --no-check to apply (rebase / fast-forward).\n\
        Per repo:\n  \
          - up-to-date        nothing to do\n  \
          - behind            fast-forward bookmark to remote\n  \
          - ahead             nothing to sync (local has unpushed work)\n  \
          - diverged          rebase local onto remote; fail on conflicts\n  \
          - no remote         bookmark has no @<remote> counterpart; skip\n\n\
        Scripts and automation should pass --check or --no-check\n\
        explicitly — defaults can shift, explicit flags lock in the\n\
        contract. Interactive use can rely on the default.\n\n\
        After a successful --no-check sync, `@` is repositioned onto\n\
        the synced bookmark: the code repo `jj new`s a clean `@` (or\n\
        rebases a dirty one with --rebase / a prompt), the `.claude`\n\
        session repo always `jj new main`s. This pass runs outside the\n\
        revert region below, so a reposition problem doesn't undo the\n\
        successful fetch / fast-forward.\n\n\
        On any failure, every repo is reverted to its starting state via\n\
        `jj op restore`. Working-copy files are preserved across the\n\
        revert — the operation log rewinds but disk content stays.\n\n\
        Output shape:\n  \
          - all-up-to-date: one-line summary (`sync: N repos, all bookmarks up-to-date`)\n  \
          - action needed (--check):  per-repo fetch + state + fatal error\n  \
          - action needed (--no-check): per-repo fetch + state + actions\n  \
          - --quiet:        no output; exit code signals success")]
    Sync(sync::SyncArgs),

    /// Squash, set bookmark, and/or push a jj repo
    #[command(long_about = "Squash, set bookmark, and/or push a jj repo.\n\n\
        Designed for the bot to atomically finalize its session repo:\n\
        --detach exits immediately, --delay waits for trailing writes,\n\
        --squash folds them in, --bookmark + --push sends it upstream.\n\
        Every flag is opt-in. See README.md for details.")]
    Finalize(finalize::FinalizeArgs),

    /// Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command
    #[command(
        long_about = "Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.\n\n\
        Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a\n\
        single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a\n\
        state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can\n\
        resume without re-doing completed stages.\n\n\

content.text:
The bug is fixed: plain `vc-x1 sync` now converges and repositions, and the regression test that encoded your t1A/t1B transcript is green and un-ignored. Work summary for review:

- **`src/sync.rs`** — the mode split is gone:
  - `--no-check` removed; the default *is* the atomic sync (fetch → fast-forward/rebase → reposition `@`).
  - `--check` kept as a **hidden** deprecated flag (verify-only) solely for push's preflight shell-out, with docs noting it was never fully read-only (fetch auto-ffs) and pointing at the rewire follow-up.
  - Module/struct/function docs rewritten around the single-operation contract; check-mode's error now says "resolve with `vc-x1 sync`"; `log_state` says "fast-forward/rebase needed" instead of "would …".
  - Auto-revert on failure is untouched here — that's `-3`'s stop-on-error work.
- **`src/sync/tests.rs`** — `parse_overrides`/`parse_defaults` updated; `parse_check_flag` now pins the hidden alias; new `parse_no_check_rejected` (a stale `--no-check` script must fail loudly, not silently change meaning).
- **`src/sync/integration_tests.rs`** — `apply_params()` → `default_params()` (12 call sites); doc tweaks.
- **`tests/cli_sync.rs`** — the default-mode test is un-ignored (primary regression test); the old `--no-check` test replaced by two: `cli_sync_check_alias_verifies_only` (pins the alias's semantics: main auto-ffs, `@` *not* repositioned) and `cli_sync_no_check_rejected`.
- **`src/main.rs`** — sync's `long_about` rewritten (no modes).
- **`src/push.rs`** — preflight doc comment updated (deprecated alias, follow-up pointer); behavior unchanged, and its `vc-x1 sync --check` shell-out still works.
- **`notes/todo.md`** markers, **`Cargo.toml`/`Cargo.lock`** → `0.67.0-2`.

fmt/clippy/test/install all clean. You may also want to hand-verify on your t1B clone: build is installed, so `vc-x1 sync` there should now land `@` on main in both repos. Ready to commit — please review.

aiTitle: Test main bookmark update on vc-x1 sync

message.content:
That touched a lot of files!

I verified it works in t1B.d, and why not remove --check if it's hidden?

content.text:
Both fair points.

**On the file count:** the semantic change is really one file (`src/sync.rs`); the rest is fallout radius — every place that *spelled* the old modes: unit tests parsing the flags, integration-test param helpers, the CLI tests, `main.rs` help text, one push doc comment. That's the cost of renaming part of a CLI contract, and it's why the commit body's file list makes it look bigger than it is: mostly docs and test renames, no behavior outside sync.

**On removing `--check` outright:** because `push` would break *today*. Its preflight stage literally shells out to the installed binary — `run("vc-x1", ["sync", "--check"], root)` at `src/push.rs:899` — so if `-2` deletes the flag, the next `vc-x1 push` (including the one that publishes this very cycle!) dies in preflight with a clap error. Making push not need it isn't a one-liner: the preflight has to become an in-process call, and there's a real design question hiding in it — should push's first stage *be* a full sync (which moves your working copy mid-push), or a classify-only refusal? That's why I parked it as Todo #3 ("push preflight in-process; drop `--check`; revisit push auto-rollback") rather than folding it into `-2`. Hidden-but-alive is the standard deprecation half-step: no new users can discover it, the one in-tree caller keeps working, and the follow-up cycle deletes both ends together.

If you'd rather not carry the alias at all, the alternative is to expand this cycle: give sync a classify-only internal function, have push call it in-process, and delete `--check` — but that drags push redesign questions into what's currently a clean, verified sync change. My recommendation: ship `-2` as reviewed, keep Todo #3 as the very next cycle after 0.67.0 closes.

Shall I write up the commit description for `-2`?

message.content: yes, write it up and push

lines: /// Sync the given repos against their remotes.
/// Orchestrates the full flow: pre-flight clean-check on every repo,
-/// snapshot each repo's current op id, then hand off to `run_plan` for
-/// fetch + classify + (optional) act. On any error, revert every repo
-/// to its snapshot op via `jj op restore` so the caller sees an atomic
-/// "either it all went through or nothing changed" outcome.
+/// snapshot each repo's current op id (persisted to
+/// `.vc-x1/sync-state.toml` per repo), then hand off to `run_plan`
+/// for fetch + classify + act, then reposition `@`.
+/// **Stop-on-error**: a failure leaves every repo exactly where the
+/// failing step stopped so the user can inspect what happened —
+/// nothing is auto-reverted. The error report names each repo's

1	//! The `sync` subcommand: fetch + classify + rebase / fast-forward
2	//! a workspace's repos against their remotes, then reposition `@`
3	//! onto the synced bookmark.
4	//!
5	//! Sync is a single atomic operation — verify-then-act happens
6	//! inside one invocation against one fetch snapshot (a separate
7	//! check-then-apply pair of runs would race the remote). The
8	//! hidden deprecated `--check` flag preserves the old verify-only
9	//! mode for `push`'s preflight until that is rewired in-process.
10	//!
11	//! **Stop-on-error**: a failure leaves state where the failing step
12	//! stopped so the user can inspect it. Each repo's pre-sync op id
13	//! is persisted to `.vc-x1/sync-state.toml` (see `state`); the
14	//! error report points at `vc-x1 revert` to undo explicitly.
15	//!
16	//! `current_op_id` / `op_restore` are `pub(crate)` so `push` (and
17	//! the `revert` subcommand) reuse the same snapshot-and-restore
18	//! primitives.
19	//!

1	//! Integration tests for the sync module.
2	//!
3	//! End-to-end tests for `sync_repos` against real dual-repo jj
4	//! fixtures. Each test builds an isolated fixture (bare-git
5	//! remotes + colocated jj repos) under a unique tempdir via
6	//! `Fixture::new`, then drives the scenario with plain `jj`
7	//! subprocess calls. Requires `jj` in `PATH`.
8	//!
9	//! Fixtures clean themselves up via `Fixture`'s `Drop` impl so a
10	//! panicking test still removes its tempdir.
11	
12	use super::*;
13	use crate::options_flags::scope::Side;
14	use crate::test_helpers::Fixture;
15	use std::fs;

20	/// Resolver helpers must consume what `init --repo-local` produces.
21	///
22	/// Builds a real dual-repo fixture (bare git remotes + colocated jj
23	/// repos + the canonical `.vc-config.toml` pair init writes), then
24	/// drives the resolver chain `sync()` uses when `-R` is empty:
25	///
26	/// - `find_workspace_root_from(&fx.claude)` walks up to `fx.work`
27	///   (proves cwd-portability against init's `path = "/.claude"` /
28	///   `path = "/"` config split).
29	/// - `default_scope(Some(&fx.work))` reads the workspace config and
30	///   resolves to the dual-repo default.
31	/// - `scope_to_repos` maps each `Scope` shape to the right
32	///   absolute path(s) under the fixture.
33	///
34	/// Pure check on the resolver chain — does not invoke `sync()`
35	/// itself, since that walks `std::env::current_dir()` and parallel
36	/// `cargo test` makes cwd mutation unsafe.
37	#[test]
38	fn resolver_chain_against_init_repo_local() {
39	    let fx = Fixture::new("resolver-chain");
40	
41	    // Walk-up from the bot side lands on the app root.
42	    assert_eq!(
43	        find_workspace_root_from(&fx.claude).as_deref(),
44	        Some(&*fx.work),
45	        "find_workspace_root should resolve from .claude up to work"
46	    );
47	    // Walk-up from the app root finds itself.
48	    assert_eq!(
49	        find_workspace_root_from(&fx.work).as_deref(),
50	        Some(&*fx.work)
51	    );
52	
53	    // init --repo-local writes other-repo = ".claude", so the
54	    // workspace's default scope is dual.
55	    assert_eq!(
56	        default_scope(Some(&fx.work)),
57	        Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot])
58	    );
59	
60	    // Each scope shape resolves to the right absolute path(s).
61	    assert_eq!(
62	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
63	        vec![fx.work.clone(), fx.claude.clone()]
64	    );
65	    assert_eq!(
66	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
67	        vec![fx.work.clone()]
68	    );
69	    assert_eq!(
70	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
71	        vec![fx.claude.clone()]
72	    );
73	
74	    // sync_repos accepts the resolved list and reports up-to-date
75	    // — the resolver's output is shaped the way sync expects.
76	    let resolved = scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap();
77	    sync_repos(&resolved, &default_params()).expect("sync should succeed on resolved repos");
78	}
79	
80	/// Run `jj <args> -R <repo>` and assert success; returns trimmed stdout.
81	fn jj(repo: &Path, args: &[&str]) -> String {
82	    let out = Command::new("jj")
83	        .args(args)
84	        .arg("-R")
85	        .arg(repo)
86	        .output()
87	        .expect("spawn jj");
88	    assert!(
89	        out.status.success(),
90	        "jj {args:?} failed in {}: {}",
91	        repo.display(),
92	        String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stderr)
93	    );
94	    String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stdout).trim().to_string()
95	}

102	    )

content.text:
`-3` is done and green (385 bin tests now, including the two new state round-trip tests). Work summary:

- **`src/sync/state.rs`** (new) — the persisted pre-sync snapshot, one file per synced repo at `<repo>/.vc-x1/sync-state.toml` (same gitignored dir as push's state): `save`/`load`/`clear` of `{version, op_id, bookmark, remote, started_at}`, flat-TOML in push's style. Per-repo rather than one combined file because sync accepts arbitrary repo lists. `load` carries an `#[allow(dead_code)]` with a note — its production caller is `-4`'s `revert`.
- **`src/sync.rs`** — the auto-revert block is gone. Snapshots are persisted before any fetch; `run_plan` + the reposition pass now share one stop-on-error region; on failure sync **leaves everything in place** and reports: the error, "no auto-revert", each repo's pre-sync op id, and both undo routes (`vc-x1 revert`, or manual `jj op restore <op> -R <repo>`). On full success the snapshots are cleared so a stale file can't become a revert target later. Docs updated (module, `sync_repos`, `run_plan`, `op_restore`).
- **`src/sync/integration_tests.rs`** — the two auto-revert tests are rewritten to the new contract: `sync_conflict_stops_and_keeps_state` and `sync_diverged_conflict_stops_and_keeps_state` assert conflicts *remain* for inspection, the state file records the pre-sync op id (for `.claude` too — the repo that synced cleanly before the failure), and a manual `op_restore` from that id cleans up (previewing what `revert` automates). `sync_up_to_date` additionally asserts success clears the state files.
- **`notes/todo.md`** marker, **`Cargo.toml`/`Cargo.lock`** → `0.67.0-3`, installed.

One behavior note worth your eyes: with stop-on-error, a *partial* failure across repos no longer rolls back the repos that already synced — the old "all or nothing" contract is replaced by "stopped where it failed, everything inspectable, revert restores all snapshots". That's the philosophy you asked for, applied cross-repo.

Ready to commit — please review.

content.content:
1	//! `SubcommandRunner` trait
2	//!
3	//! The purpose is to simplify invoking a subcommand so `main`
4	//! can invoke any subcommand with one line:
5	//!
6	//! `Commands::Chid(args) => args.dispatch(&ctx),`
7	//!
8	//! Instead of the ~11 lines that the default `dispatch` below
9	//! now encapsulates.
10	//!
11	//! Implementors provide:
12	//!
13	//! - [`SubcommandRunner::to_params`] — build the clap-free
14	//!   `Params` from the clap `Args` (covers both `From` /
15	//!   `TryFrom` shapes uniformly via `Result<_, String>`).
16	//! - [`SubcommandRunner::run`] — the subcommand body.
17	//!
18	//! The default [`SubcommandRunner::dispatch`] builds `Params`
19	//! via `to_params`, runs via `run`, and maps the result to
20	//! `ExitCode`. Errors at any stage log via `error!` and return
21	//! `ExitCode::FAILURE`. `Context` is loaded once in `main` and
22	//! passed in by reference so a single `Context` is shared across
23	//! the (one) match arm that actually runs.
24	//!
25	//! ## See also
26	//!
27	//! - The wider CLI-args / subcommand-layer split this trait
28	//!   formalizes: [`ARCHITECTURE.md > args → Context + Params`][arch].
29	//! - Cycle design, ladder, evaluation gate:
30	//!   [`chores-10.md > 0.50.0-0`][open].
31	//! - Worked example, trait-shape decisions, naming:
32	//!   [`chores-10.md > 0.50.0-1`][port].
33	//! - First implementor: [`crate::chid`].
34	//!
35	//! [arch]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md#args--context--params
36	//! [open]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/blob/main/notes/chores/chores-10.md#chore-open-subcommand-trait-sweep-0500-0
37	//! [port]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/blob/main/notes/chores/chores-10.md#refactor-subcommandrunner-trait--chid-0500-1
38	
39	use std::process::ExitCode;
40	
41	use log::error;
42	
43	use crate::context::Context;
44	
45	/// Trait implemented by every subcommand's clap `Args` type so
46	/// `main.rs` can dispatch via a single `args.dispatch(&ctx)`
47	/// call.
48	pub trait SubcommandRunner {
49	    /// The clap-free `Params` struct the subcommand body
50	    /// consumes.
51	    type Params;
52	
53	    /// Build `Params` from `Args`. `Result<_, String>` absorbs
54	    /// both `From` (return `Ok(...)`) and `TryFrom` (forward
55	    /// the error) shapes uniformly.
56	    fn to_params(&self) -> Result<Self::Params, String>;
57	
58	    /// Run the subcommand body.
59	    fn run(ctx: &Context, params: &Self::Params) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>;
60	
61	    /// Default dispatch: build `Params` via `to_params`, bracket
62	    /// the run with `crate::bm_track` enter/exit, execute via
63	    /// `run`, and map the result to `ExitCode`. Errors at any
64	    /// stage log via `error!` and return `ExitCode::FAILURE`.
65	    /// `bm_track` itself emits at `debug!`, so default runs stay
66	    /// quiet and the detached `finalize --exec` child (which runs
67	    /// without `-v`) silently no-ops — no per-call gate is needed
68	    /// here. `surface_previous_failures` for finalize's failure
69	    /// markers is handled in `main` before dispatch, so dispatch
70	    /// has no session-chrome responsibility at all anymore.
71	    fn dispatch(&self, ctx: &Context) -> ExitCode {
72	        let params = match self.to_params() {
73	            Ok(p) => p,
74	            Err(e) => {
75	                error!("{e}");
76	                return ExitCode::FAILURE;
77	            }
78	        };
79	
80	        // Command name is the first positional after the binary;
81	        // clap has already validated it by the time we reach
82	        // dispatch (top-level parse errors exit earlier).
83	        let command_name = std::env::args().nth(1).unwrap_or_else(|| "?".to_string()); // OK: default when somehow invoked without a subcommand
84	
85	        crate::bm_track("enter", &command_name);
86	        let exit_code = match Self::run(ctx, &params) {
87	            Ok(()) => ExitCode::SUCCESS,
88	            Err(e) => {
89	                error!("{e}");
90	                ExitCode::FAILURE
91	            }
92	        };
93	        crate::bm_track("exit ", &command_name);
94	        exit_code
95	    }
96	}

41	/// Banner string emitted as the first line of normal command runs
42	/// and shown at the top of subcommand `--help` output. Built from
43	/// `Cargo.toml`'s name + version at compile time so it stays in
44	/// sync with the bumped version.
45	const BANNER: &str = concat!(env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"), " ", env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"));
46	
47	/// Top-level about line — name, version, and the project tagline
48	/// on a single line. Used as the top-level `about` so `vc-x1 -h`
49	/// reads as one banner-plus-tagline header instead of two stacked
50	/// lines.
51	const TOP_ABOUT: &str = concat!(
52	    env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"),
53	    " ",
54	    env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"),
55	    " - jj workspace tooling"
56	);
57	
58	/// Build the clap command tree with `BANNER` set as `before_help`
59	/// on every subcommand (transitively). Top-level skips `before_help`
60	/// because its own `about` already carries the name+version+tagline.
61	/// Walks via `mut_subcommand` so individual subcommand
62	/// `#[command(long_about = ...)]` blocks don't have to repeat the
63	/// banner text.
64	fn cli_with_banner() -> clap::Command {
65	    fn add_to_subs(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
66	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
67	            .get_subcommands()
68	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
69	            .collect();
70	        for name in names {
71	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
72	        }
73	        cmd
74	    }
75	    fn add_with_banner(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
76	        cmd = cmd.before_help(BANNER);
77	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
78	            .get_subcommands()
79	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
80	            .collect();
81	        for name in names {
82	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
83	        }
84	        cmd
85	    }
86	    add_to_subs(Cli::command())
87	}

99	    /// one invocation.

1	//! Integration tests for the sync module.
2	//!
3	//! End-to-end tests for `sync_repos` against real dual-repo jj
4	//! fixtures. Each test builds an isolated fixture (bare-git
5	//! remotes + colocated jj repos) under a unique tempdir via
6	//! `Fixture::new`, then drives the scenario with plain `jj`
7	//! subprocess calls. Requires `jj` in `PATH`.
8	//!
9	//! Fixtures clean themselves up via `Fixture`'s `Drop` impl so a
10	//! panicking test still removes its tempdir.
11	
12	use super::*;
13	use crate::options_flags::scope::Side;
14	use crate::test_helpers::Fixture;
15	use std::fs;
16	use std::process::Command;
17	
18	use crate::common::{default_scope, find_workspace_root_from, scope_to_repos};
19	
20	/// Resolver helpers must consume what `init --repo-local` produces.
21	///
22	/// Builds a real dual-repo fixture (bare git remotes + colocated jj
23	/// repos + the canonical `.vc-config.toml` pair init writes), then
24	/// drives the resolver chain `sync()` uses when `-R` is empty:
25	///
26	/// - `find_workspace_root_from(&fx.claude)` walks up to `fx.work`
27	///   (proves cwd-portability against init's `path = "/.claude"` /
28	///   `path = "/"` config split).
29	/// - `default_scope(Some(&fx.work))` reads the workspace config and
30	///   resolves to the dual-repo default.
31	/// - `scope_to_repos` maps each `Scope` shape to the right
32	///   absolute path(s) under the fixture.
33	///
34	/// Pure check on the resolver chain — does not invoke `sync()`
35	/// itself, since that walks `std::env::current_dir()` and parallel
36	/// `cargo test` makes cwd mutation unsafe.
37	#[test]
38	fn resolver_chain_against_init_repo_local() {
39	    let fx = Fixture::new("resolver-chain");
40	
41	    // Walk-up from the bot side lands on the app root.
42	    assert_eq!(
43	        find_workspace_root_from(&fx.claude).as_deref(),
44	        Some(&*fx.work),
45	        "find_workspace_root should resolve from .claude up to work"
46	    );
47	    // Walk-up from the app root finds itself.
48	    assert_eq!(
49	        find_workspace_root_from(&fx.work).as_deref(),
50	        Some(&*fx.work)
51	    );
52	
53	    // init --repo-local writes other-repo = ".claude", so the
54	    // workspace's default scope is dual.
55	    assert_eq!(
56	        default_scope(Some(&fx.work)),
57	        Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot])
58	    );
59	
60	    // Each scope shape resolves to the right absolute path(s).
61	    assert_eq!(
62	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
63	        vec![fx.work.clone(), fx.claude.clone()]
64	    );
65	    assert_eq!(
66	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
67	        vec![fx.work.clone()]
68	    );
69	    assert_eq!(
70	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
71	        vec![fx.claude.clone()]
72	    );
73	
74	    // sync_repos accepts the resolved list and reports up-to-date
75	    // — the resolver's output is shaped the way sync expects.
76	    let resolved = scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap();
77	    sync_repos(&resolved, &default_params()).expect("sync should succeed on resolved repos");
78	}

80	/// Run `jj <args> -R <repo>` and assert success; returns trimmed stdout.
81	fn jj(repo: &Path, args: &[&str]) -> String {
82	    let out = Command::new("jj")
83	        .args(args)
84	        .arg("-R")
85	        .arg(repo)
86	        .output()
87	        .expect("spawn jj");
88	    assert!(
89	        out.status.success(),
90	        "jj {args:?} failed in {}: {}",
91	        repo.display(),
92	        String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stderr)
93	    );
94	    String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stdout).trim().to_string()
95	}

102	    )

133	    !jj(

136	            "log",

148	/// Add a local-only commit on `main` in `repo` (not pushed), then
149	/// restore `@` to an empty child so pre-flight still passes.
150	///
151	/// Sequence: write file → describe `@` → advance `main` to `@` →
152	/// create a fresh empty `@` above it.
153	fn add_local_commit(repo: &Path, file: &str, content: &str, msg: &str) {
154	    fs::write(repo.join(file), content).expect("write local file");
155	    jj(repo, &["describe", "@", "-m", msg]);
156	    jj(repo, &["bookmark", "set", "main", "-r", "@"]);
157	    jj(repo, &["new"]);
158	}

160	/// Clone `remote_url` into `<base>/<work_name>` (colocated) and
161	/// return the new workdir.
162	fn clone(base: &Path, remote_url: &Path, work_name: &str) -> PathBuf {
163	    let workdir = base.join(work_name);
164	    let out = Command::new("jj")
165	        .args(["git", "clone", "--colocate"])
166	        .arg(remote_url)
167	        .arg(&workdir)
168	        .output()

182	fn push_from_clone(

187	    content: &str,

content.text:
`0.67.0-4` is complete — `vc-x1 revert` exists and the whole inspect-then-undo loop is covered end-to-end. Work summary:

- **`src/revert.rs`** (new) — the `revert` subcommand on the standard `SubcommandRunner` pattern. `-R`/`--scope` resolve exactly like sync (shared resolver, below), so a failed `vc-x1 sync` and the following `vc-x1 revert` name the same repos. Per repo with a snapshot: log the op id + what/when it was syncing, `jj op restore`, clear the state file. Snapshot-less repos are skipped with a note (normal post-success condition); zero snapshots anywhere is an error — as the smoke test above shows. A mid-way restore failure stops (same philosophy), leaving remaining snapshots for a re-run.
- **`src/sync.rs`** — `resolve_params_to_repos` generalized to `pub(crate) resolve_repos(repo, scope)` shared by both subcommands.
- **`src/sync/state.rs`** — the two `#[allow(dead_code)]` bridges removed now that `revert` is the production reader.
- **`src/main.rs`** — `revert` registered (module, `Commands::Revert` with long_about, dispatch arm); also fixed sync's `long_about`, which I'd left describing the old auto-revert in `-3` — it now states stop-on-error + `vc-x1 revert`.
- **`src/sync/integration_tests.rs`** — new `revert_restores_after_failed_sync`: conflict fixture → sync fails → `revert_repos` restores both repos (including `.claude`, which had synced cleanly), `main`/`main@origin` back to pre-sync, no conflicts, state cleared, and a second revert errors with "nothing to revert".
- Parse unit tests in `revert.rs`; version `0.67.0-4` installed.

388 bin tests + all CLI suites green. Ready to commit — please review.
